coredata.dc:title: A general approach to 3D porous CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) for remarkable performance hybrid supercapacitors
coredata.dc:creator: Wei, Guijuan
Zhao, Xixia
Du, Kun
Wang, Zhaojie
Liu, Ming
Zhang, Shuo
Wang, Shutao
Zhang, Jun
An, Changhua
coredata.dc:description: To date, zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new carbon nanomaterials, have attracted attention in the improvement of the electrical conductivity for electrode materials. However, few attempts have been made to the use of CQDs as the structure-directing agent for the assembly of functional micro/nanoelectrodes. Herein, we have developed a general, and simple strategy to fabricate 3D porous hierarchical carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) composite nanostructures from thermolysis of corresponding CQDs/M(OH)y, where CQDs play crucial role as a structure-directing agent in tuning the morphologies of the M(OH)y. With the merits of the large electroactive surface area, superior electronic conductivity of CQDs, and fruitful porous structure, the as-fabricated battery-type electrode materials present amazing supercapacitor performance. For example, the as-made porous hierarchical CQDs/Co3O4 nanoarchitectures exhibited remarkable electrochemical performance with 1603Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, excellent rate capacity of 70.6% from 1 to 100Ag−1, superior cycling ability (97.0% capacity remained after 2000 cycles). Furthermore, the CQDs/Co3O4//AC hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) exhibits a high specific capacity of 210.4Fg−1 with 74.8Whkg−1. The present work can be enlarged for the design of other functional 3D hierarchical micro-nanostructures in the fields of energy storage, catalysis, and photo/electrocatalysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: General method
CQDs
Self-assembly
3D porous structures
Energy storage

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of 3D porous hybridized CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) and 3D MxOy micro-nanostructures
Preparation of electrodes and electrochemical measurements

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of the as-prepared samples: (a) CQDs/Co3O4, (b) CQDs/NiCo2O4, and (c) CQDs/NiO.
FESEM images of MxOy: (a) Co3O4, (c) NiCo2O4, (e) NiO and the CQDs/MxOy: (b) CQDs/Co3O4, (d) CQDs/NiCo2O4, (f) CQDs/NiO.
TEM and HRTEM images of (a, b) CQDs/Co3O4, (c, d) CQDs/NiCo2O4, and (e, f) CQDs/NiO.

XPS of CQDs/Co3O4: (a) survey spectrum, (b) C 1s core-level, (c) Co 2p core-level, and (d) Nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherm with the corresponding BJH pore size distribution curve (the inset in d) of CQDs/Co3O4 nanocomposites.

CV curves of (a) Co3O4 and (c, e) CQDs/Co3O4 composite electrode at various scan rates. Galvanostatic discharge curves of (b) Co3O4 and (d, f) CQDs/Co3O4 composite electrode at various current densities. The inset in (e) is the kinetic analyses of Co3O4 and CQDs/Co3O4 (the Ip versus v 1/2).
Specific capacitance of the electrodes calculated from different current densities over (a) CQDs/MxOy and (b) MxOy, (c) Specific capacitance of the MxOy and CQDs/MxOy electrodes at 1Ag−1.

(a) EIS spectra of Co3O4 and CQDs/Co3O4, and the inset is the equivalent electrical circuit. (b) Cycling performance of CQDs/MxOy (Co, Ni) electrodes measured at a current density of 100Ag−1 after 2000 cycles.

(a) CV curves of the CQDs/Co3O4//AC in different voltage windows at a scan rate of 50mVs−1. (b) CV curves and (c) GCD curves of the assembled HSC at different scan rates or cur-rent densities with a 1.6V voltage window. (d) The specific capacitance of the CQDs/Co3O4//AC HSC at various current densities. (e) Ragone plots of the as-prepared CQDs/Co3O4//AC HSC and the inset shows the schematic illustration of the constitution of the hybrid device. (f) Cycling stability of the CQDs/Co3O4//AC HSC at a high current density of 20Ag−1.

head.ce:title: A general approach to 3D porous CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) for remarkable performance hybrid supercapacitors

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing, College of Science, and College of Chemical Engineering, China, University of Petroleum, Qingdao 266580, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Heavy Oil Processing
College of Science, and College of Chemical Engineering, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Tianjin University of Technology, Tianjin 300384, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Tianjin Key Laboratory of Organic Solar Cells and Photochemical Conversion College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To date, zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new carbon nanomaterials, have attracted attention in the improvement of the electrical conductivity for electrode materials. However, few attempts have been made to the use of CQDs as the structure-directing agent for the assembly of functional micro/nanoelectrodes. Herein, we have developed a general, and simple strategy to fabricate 3D porous hierarchical carbon quantum dots (CQDs)/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) composite nanostructures from thermolysis of corresponding CQDs/M(OH)y, where CQDs play crucial role as a structure-directing agent in tuning the morphologies of the M(OH)y. With the merits of the large electroactive surface area, superior electronic conductivity of CQDs, and fruitful porous structure, the as-fabricated battery-type electrode materials present amazing supercapacitor performance. For example, the as-made porous hierarchical CQDs/Co3O4 nanoarchitectures exhibited remarkable electrochemical performance with 1603Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, excellent rate capacity of 70.6% from 1 to 100Ag−1, superior cycling ability (97.0% capacity remained after 2000 cycles). Furthermore, the CQDs/Co3O4//AC hybrid supercapacitor (HSC) exhibits a high specific capacity of 210.4Fg−1 with 74.8Whkg−1. The present work can be enlarged for the design of other functional 3D hierarchical micro-nanostructures in the fields of energy storage, catalysis, and photo/electrocatalysis.

ce:section.ce:para: As the increasing concerns on the energy crisis and environmental issue, great efforts have been devoted to developing sustainable and environmentally friendly alternative energy sources [1–3]. Supercapacitors (SCs) are gaining tremendous interest due to their excellent power density, fast charge and discharge, and excellent cycle stability [4–8]. Hybrid supercapacitors (HSCs) comprising a battery-type Faradaic electrode (typically transition metal oxides and hydroxides) and a capacitor-type electrical double-layer electrode have drawn great attention in recent years as they exhibit high specific capacity and energy densities [7,8]. It is well known that the battery-type electrode is the key component in achieving high-performance HSCs. Designing the battery-type electrode materials with large surface area, high conductivity, and mechanical stability is of paramount importance to meet their requirements.

Three dimensional (3D) porous nanoarchitectures with high surface area, and well-defined pathways for electrolyte access are a class of promising electrode architectures for high-performance HSCs [9,10]. As a result, there has been an increasing demand for simple and controllable synthesis of 3D structured materials. An attractive concept to enhance the storage density of the hybrid supercapacitors using battery-type positive electrode materials is to achieve smart 3D porous structures, which are usually formed by the assembly of low dimensional nanostructures. For example, Xiao reported that 3D urchin-like hierarchical Co3O4 exhibited 781Fg−1 (0.5Ag−1) [11]. Liu et al. prepared 3D hierarchical Co3O4@Co3S4 nanoarrays for SCs with 1284.3Fg−1 at 3mVs−1 [12]. However, synthesizing 3D porous structures as efficient electrode materials by a general and simple strategy is still challenging.

Over the last two decades, transition metal oxides (MxOy, M=Co, Ni, etc.) have been intensively exploited as electrode materials because of their various oxidation states for redox reaction [13–15]. These unique properties afford them to be used in the energy storage and conversion. Unfortunately, relatively slow redox reaction kinetics, poor conductivity and structural collapse still limit full electrochemical reactions, resulting in relatively low electrical energy density and fast capacity decay. Various approaches, such as controlling nanostructures [16,17] and integrating carbon [18,19] have been developed to improve their electrochemical performance. Hybridizing MxOy with conductive carbon provides an effective approach for enhancing electrochemical performance and concurrently improving the cycling stability. As an attractive material, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is an excellent candidate for its quantum confinement and edge effects [20,21].

Here, we have designed a universal hydrothermal method to prepare 3D porous CQDs/MxOy nanocomposites assembling by low dimensional nanoblocks, where CQDs serving as a structure-directing agent. In the reaction system, the “electrostatic forces” between CQDs and cations is responsible for the formation of 3D porous hierarchical nanostructures. A series of CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) such as CQDs/Co3O4, CQDs/NiCo2O4, CQDs/NiO have been achieved by simply varying the metal precursors. Electrochemical tests with conventional three-electrode and two electrode system show that the optimized 3D porous hierarchical CQDs/MxOy nanocomposites with high surface areas and mesopore distributions exhibit superior electrochemical performance and remarkable stability.
NaOH was purchased from Xilong Chemical Co., Ltd. (Guangdong, China). Ethanol, Ni(Ac)2·4H2O, Co(Ac)2·4H2O and urea were purchased as analytical reagents from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (China).

Taking Co3O4 as a typical example, 1mmol Co(Ac)2·4H2O and 4mmol urea were successively added to 30mL of deionized water. After they were dissolved, the mixture was transferred to the autoclave and kept at 150°C for 6h. The red powder was harvested after being washed and dried at 50°C overnight. Then the as-obtained samples were annealed 3h in air at 300°C with an increasing rate of 1°C/min. For the synthesis of NiCo2O4 and NiO, similar methods were used except that Ni(Ac)2·4H2O (0.3mmol) and Co(Ac)2·4H2O (0.6mmol), Ni(Ac)2·4H2O (1mmol) were separately used as metal sources.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of 3D porous hybridized CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) and 3D MxOy micro-nanostructures
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were firstly prepared by electrochemically anodic oxidation of alcohol as previously reported [22]. The following procedure was similar to the above metal oxide nanostructures except that the reaction solution was switched to be 15mL aqueous solution of 2mg/mL CQDs. For comparison, the obtained CQDs/Co3O4 sample was annealed at 600°C for 60min in air to burn out CQDs. The product was denoted as 3D Co3O4 .

An X-ray powder diffractometer (XRD, Siemens D5005) was used to characterize the structures of hybrid materials. The shapes and microstructures were characterized on a field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM, Hitachi S-480) and a high resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL JEM-2100UHR), respectively. Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area was measured with a Micromeritics ASAP 2020 nitrogen adsorption apparatus by N2 physisorption. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were measured on an ESCALAB 250Xi system (Thermo Scientific) with Al K radiation. Raman spectra were recorded on a Raman spectrometer (DXR Microscope).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of electrodes and electrochemical measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
A slurry was prepared by mixing the as-prepared CQDs/Co3O4, acetylene black, and PVDF with a mass ratio of 80:15:5, and dried at 60°C overnight. 2mg of the mixture was coated onto a nickel foam in 1cm2 to obtain a final working electrode. The active material loading was 1.6mg. Cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge-discharge (GCD) measurements were performed on an electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua, CHI 760E). An Hg/HgO and Pt plate (15mm*15mm) were used as the reference and counter electrode, respectively. The electrochemical impedance measurements (EIS) were carried out at 5mV ac oscillation amplitude from 0.01Hz to 100kHz. The specific capacity (C) was calculated by the following equations:
C =
2
i
m
∫ Vdt
V
2
|
Vi
Vf
where im
=I/m (A g−1) is the constant discharge current, V is the initial (Vi) and final (Vf) potential value, m is the mass (g) of the active material, respectively.

The electrochemical performance of HSCs were also measured by using CV and GCD measurements. The anode electrode was prepared by mixing 95wt% of AC and 5wt% of PVDF, and the CQDs/MxOy electrode was used as the cathode electrode. The fabricated HSC with the optimized mass ratio was measured at different current densities to evaluate the energy density and the power density. The specific capacity (C,Fg−1), energy density (E, Wh kg−1) and power density (P, Wkg−1) were calculated from chronopotentiometric curves according to the following equations, respectively.
C =
2
i
m
∫ Vdt
V
2
|
Vi
Vf
E =
I ∫ Vdt
m
P =
E
Δ t
where im
=I/m (A g−1) is the constant discharge current, V is the initial (Vi) and final (Vf) potential value, m is the mass (g) of the electrode material, respectively.

Fig. 1 shows the XRD patterns of the hybridized nanocomposites of CQDs/Co3O4, CQDs/NiCo2O4, and CQD/NiO. The pattern of CQDs/Co3O4 reveals the typical diffraction peaks of the cubic phase of Co3O4 (JCPDS No. 09-0418). For the other as-obtained two samples, the corresponding patterns could be assigned to the cubic phase of NiCo2O4 (JCPDS No. 01-073-1702), and cubic phase of NiO (JCPDS No. 03-065-2901), respectively. The average crystallite sizes of CQDs/Co3O4, CQDs/NiCo2O4, and CQDs/NiO are estimated to be 19.0nm, 12.3nm, and 11.3nm according to the Scherrer equation. However, the diffraction peak for graphite (002) plane is absent in all the samples, indicating the low content and crystallinity of CQDs.

SEM was used to investigate the morphologies of the as-synthesized 3D porous hierarchical structures. Taking CQDs/Co3O4 as a typical example, when CQDs were absent, the obtained bare Co3O4 displayed irregular microflakes with different sizes (Fig.2 a, b). However, once the presence of CQDs participated, the sample displayed ultrathin nanosheets interconnected and assembly of hierarchical porous flowers, showing that CQDs have a significant effect on the shape of the sample. It is noted that the thickness of the nanosheets is much thinner than pure Co3O4, indicating that CQDs/Co3O4 hierarchical nanocomposite may expose more active sites. The corresponding EDS elemental mapping (Fig. S1) reveals that Co, O and C are evenly distributed in the hierarchical nanocomposites and the mass percentage of the CQDs is determined to be 4.72 wt%. The strategy can also be extended to other metal oxides, such as CQDs/NiCo2O4 and CQDs/NiO. As shown in Fig.2c, d, the NiCo2O4 nanowires are successfully synthesized with lengths as long as several micrometers. With the assistance of CQDs, the nanowires were self-assembled into urchin-like acanthospheres with a diameter of 1–3μm. For the NiO (Fig.2e, f), it also turns out to be 3D porous hierarchical structure made of NiO ultrathin nanosheets. These results indicate that this method provides an effective, simple and general way to get 3D porous hierarchical nanomaterials. The possible growth mechanism of the CQDs/MxOy proposed as follows: When metal salts and urea were added into the CQDs suspension, strong electrostatic interaction between My+ ions and CQDs occurs owing to the negative charged property of CQDs (Zeta potential of the pristine CQDs was −4.8mV). The strong electrostatic interaction drives them to combine each other to form 3D porous hierarchical structures as we have previously reported [23].

TEM analyses further reveal that the CQDs/Co3O4 is composed of nanosheets associated with numerous mesopores generated concurrently (Fig.3 a). HRTEM image shown in Fig.3b presents clear lattice spacing of 0.24nm, assigning to the (311) planes of Co3O4. TEM images (Fig.3c, d) confirm the porous feature of the as-prepared CQDs/NiCo2O4. It is very interesting that the CQDs/NiCo2O4 displayed hollow microspheres with a diameter of 2.5μm, consisting of nanowires with the lengths of 1μm and the average diameter of 9nm. The HRTEM image (Fig.3d) demonstrates that an individual NiCo2O4 nanowire has uniform lattice fringes. The lattice spacing is calculated to be 0.28nm, agreeing well with the (220) planes of the NiCo2O4. The as-obtained porous hierarchical CQDs/NiO architectures with a diameter of 3μm are also made of ultrathin nanosheets according to the TEM and HRTEM images (Fig.3e, f). The lattice spacing (Fig.3f) is 0.24nm, which belongs to the (111) plane of NiO. In addition, TEM image of CQDs in Fig. S2 reveals their spherical shape with diameter of 3nm and the HRTEM displays a lattice fringe of 0.21nm, which is in accord with the (100) plane of CQDs. The lattice spacing of 0.21nm is for the (100) plane of CQDs (Fig.3b, d and f) that is also detected in Fig. S2, indicating the CQDs have been incorporated in the CQDs/MxOy nanocomposites.

Fig. 4 gives XPS of CQDs/Co3O4 to demonstrate its chemical composition and the oxidation states. The survey spectrum displays the presence of Co, O and C elements (Fig.4a). The high-resolution C1s spectrum (Fig.4b) signifies a number of functional groups, such as hydroxyl (OH) and carboxyl (COO) groups, besides sp2 hybridized carbon in CQDs, which may bind to Co3O4 nanosheets through covalent force or hydrogen bonding. The binding energy at 779.9eV and its satellite peak correspond to Co 2p3/2(Fig.4c), whereas the peak at 795.3eV and its satellite peak are for the Co 2p1/2 level. A spin-energy at 15.4eV is the characteristic Co3O4 phase, which is congruent well with the XRD results. The porous Co3O4 and CQDs/Co3O4 are further confirmed by BET. As shown in Fig.4d, both bare Co3O4 and CQDs/Co3O4 nanocomposites displayed Type IV isotherms of nitrogen adsorption-desorption profile, indicating the formation of the mesporous structure. The specific surface area of CQDs/Co3O4 is determined to be 158.46m2 g−1, which is significantly higher than single Co3O4 (25.53m2 g−1) and CQDs (25.3m2 g−1, Fig. S3b) and close to Co3O4 (147.7m2 g−1, Fig. S3b). The inset of Fig.4d shows their pore-size distributions from BJH method (Barrett-Joyner-Halenda). It reveals that abundant mesopores with pore size distribution (PSD) range from 2 to 15nm in hierarchical CQDs/Co3O4 micro-nanostructures. Therefore, the unique 3D porous CQDs/Co3O4 micro-nanostructures endow it continuous ion diffusion pathways and short ion transport associated with excellent rate performance and cycling stability. Raman spectra of the pristine Co3O4 and CQDs/Co3O4 (Fig. S3a) show four characteristic peaks at 483, 523, 621, 694cm−1, assigning to the Eg,
F
2 g
1
,
F
2 g
2
, and A1g modes of Co3O4, respectively. Additionally, two new peaks at 1335cm−1 and 1580cm−1 can be attributed to the D band and G band of the CQDs, which is consistent with the CQDs synthesized by Li et al. [24]. The strong peak at around 1580cm−1 corresponds to the E2g mode of the graphite and is related to the vibration of the sp2-bonded carbon atoms in a two-dimensional hexagonal lattice. The weak peak at around 1335cm−1 is associated with the vibrations of carbon atoms with dangling bonds in the termination plane of disordered graphite. The results are corresponding to the XPS results.

Fig.6 a plotted the improved specific capacities of the CQDs/MxOy and MxOy electrodes based on the GCD curves. The specific capacities of the CQDs/Co3O4 composite are 1603, 1434, 1358, 1285, 1160 and 1132Fg−1 under 1, 10, 20, 30, 50 and 100Ag−1, respectively. The capacity retention rate is 84.7%, 72.4% and 70.6% at 20, 50 and 100Ag−1, respectively. To further evaluate the practical application of the MxOy electrodes, we also tested the specific capacity over the other samples of MxOy and the CQDs/ MxOy (Figs. S4, S5). The corresponding specific capacities are 1446 and 1329.6Fg−1 at 1Ag−1 over CQDs/NiCo2O4 and CQDs/NiO, respectively. Compared to the reported MxOy electrodes, these values are comparable or even higher [30–37]. In contrast, the specific capacities over bare NiCo2O4 and NiO are only 940Fg−1 and 792.6Fg−1 (1Ag−1), which are also much lower than their nanocomposite electrodes (Fig.6c). Moreover, the capacity retention rates reach 73.2% and 69.6% at 100Ag−1 over CQDs/NiCo2O4 and CQDs/NiO, respectively (Fig.6a). And the capacity retention rate is only 59.1%, 51.1% and 50.0% at 20Ag−1 over bare Co3O4, NiCo2O4, and NiO, respectively (Fig.6b). These results further confirms the remarkable rate capability of the 3D hierarchical structured nanocomposites. As we all know, ion diffusion and electron transport play crucial parts on the supercapacitor performances. Usually, the larger surface area and the rich channels for electron transport make porous materials as a class of efficient electrodes in energy storage field [16,38,39].

Fig.7 a presented Typical Nyquist plots of the electrodes made of Co3O4 and CQDs/Co3O4. The inset showed equivalent cyclic circuit, obtained by simulating the Impedance data of the composite with a ZView software. The net impedance is the sum of the solution resistance (Rs) and the charge transfer resistance (Rct) of the materials. The diameters of the semicircles in high-frequency region yield the Rct at the electrolyte interface, which reflected the conductivity of the electrode. It is showing that the Rct of the CQDs/Co3O4 (1.94Ω) exhibits smaller than pristine Co3O4 (5.23Ω). Obviously, the CQDs make a positive effect on decreasing the electrochemical impedance of the materials. The results are in agreement with the analyses of CV. Moreover, the CQDs/Co3O4 shows a more steep line, indicating its high capability of ion diffusion. Therefore, the 3D hierarchical CQDs/Co3O4 is an excellent electrode candidate for supercapacitors. In addition, the stability of the electrode is also very important in HSCs. Fig.7b shows the specific capacities of the MxOy/CQDs after cycling 2000 times. It can be observed that 97.0%, 95.7%, 96.1% of specific capacity can be maintained over the CQDs/Co3O4, CQDs/NiCo2O4, and CQDs/NiO electrodes, revealing their excellent electrochemical stability. In addition, just like Co3O4/CQDs, the coulombic efficiency remains 99.5% during the cycling process.

3D Co3O4 and CQDs samples were also rationally prepared to study the effect of CQD on electrochemical performance. The CV, GCD and EIS were tested to examine the electrochemical performance of the 3D Co3O4 and the CQDs. As shown in Fig. S6a, CV comparisons of Co3O4, CQDs/Co3O4, 3D Co3O4 and CQDs were performed at a scan rate of 5mVs−1. The integrated areas of the 3D Co3O4 is much large than the pristine Co3O4, meaning the 3D Co3O4 electrode possesses the higher supercapacitive performance since its uniform 3D porous micro-nanostructure facilitates the transport and diffusion of electrolyte ions. Similar behavior can also be derived from gravimetric capacitance over Co3O4, CQDs/Co3O4, 3D Co3O4 and CQDs at a current density of 1Ag−1 (Fig. S6b). According to the galvanostatic discharge profiles, the specific capacity was separately determined to be 849, 1603, and 1383Fg−1, 84Fg−1 over Co3O4, CQDs/Co3O4, 3D Co3O4 and CQDs at 1Ag−1 (Fig. S6c). Thus the CQDs/Co3O4 electrode presents better capacitance than pristine Co3O4 and 3D Co3O4, which matches the results of CV. Fig. S6d presented typical Nyquist plots of the Co3O4 and CQDs electrodes. In comparison with Co3O4 and 3D Co3O4, smaller semicircle diameter of CQDs/Co3O4 demonstrates its better electrochemical performance. Thus the presence of CQDs promotes the charge transfer capacity. Moreover, in the low-frequency region, the CQDs/Co3O4 and 3D Co3O4 manifest a more steep line leaning to an imaginary axis than pristine Co3O4, suggesting a better ion diffusion capability. The effect of CQD on supercapacitors performance can be understood as follows: (1) The 3D porous composite hierarchical structures increase the interface contact of the electrode/electrolyte and provide plentiful active sites for electrochemical reactions, leading to the enhancement of the specific capacity. (2) The presence of CQDs can enhance the electronic conductivity of the CQDs/Co3O4 composites [40,41], which could improve the transfer of electrons and facilitates the electrochemical reaction (Fig. S7), thus enhancing the electrochemical performance. (3) The functional groups and active sites of CQDs afford the electrode wettability and electrochemical reaction, giving rise to the high specific capacity and energy density. Finally, the 3D porous structure and uniform distribution of CQDs could benefit to the stable electrochemical reaction and the alleviated volume changes.

To further evaluate the down-to-earth application of the as-obtained CQDs/Co3O4, the HSC was fabricated with the CQDs/Co3O4 nanocomposite as the cathode electrode and AC as the anode electrode, respectively. The inset of Fig.8 e shows a schematic illustration of the hybrid device. Prior to assembling, the CV of both CQDs/Co3O4 electrodes and AC electrodes was compared at 20mVs−1 in a three-electrode system (Fig. S8). In Fig.8a, a series of CV curves with various voltage at 50mVs−1 were used to assess the optimal operating potential of HSC, confirming that the 1.6 V is a stable potential window. Fig.8b gives the CV curves of the assembled HSC at 5–100mVs−1 within 0–1.6V. It is noting that the contributions from electric double-layer capacity and battery-like characteristics were evidently observed in the scan rates. In addition, the current was increased with the scan rates, similar to the case of the three-electrode configuration. To prove the electrochemical performance of the CQDs/Co3O4//AC HSC, Fig.8c presented the GCD curves at 1 to 20Ag−1. The slight nonlinearity of the GCD curves is due to the redox reaction of metal ions, which is consistent with the CV results. Fig.8d plotted the calculated specific capacity at various current densities. And the corresponding specific capacities of the CQDs/Co3O4//AC HSC device reach 210.4, 197.7, 178.3, 171.5, 161.4, 150.9, 148.1Fg−1 at 1, 2, 3, 5, 10, 15, 20Ag−1, respectively (Fig.8d). At 1Ag−1, an energy density of 74.8 Wh kg−1 can be achieved at 810.0Wkg−1, which are even higher than previously reported examples [31,42–46]. As the current density increased, the specific energy is slightly decreased and the specific power increased (Table S1). It still maintains 52.7Whkg−1 even at 16202.4Wkg−1 (Fig.8e). Moreover, they also show high cycling performance measured at 20Ag−1 even after 4000 cycles (Fig.8f). The capacity loss is only 14.3%, suggesting the superior electrochemical stability of the HSC.

In conclusion, we have developed a simple and universal strategy for the fabrication of 3D porous hierarchical structures of CQDs/MxOy (M=Co, Ni) nanocomposites. CQDs are used as a structure-directing agent to induce hydrothermal self-assembly low dimension nanostructures to 3D hierarchical structure with enhanced surface area and suitable mesoporous distribution. The as-prepared CQDs/MxOy shows exceptional electrochemical performance as advanced electrodes for HSCs. For example, CQDs/Co3O4 delivers a highest capacity of 1603Fg−1 at 1Ag−1, superior rate performance (capacity retention rate reaches 70.6% of initial specific capacity at 100Ag−1), and excellent stability (over 2000 cycling, it remained 97.0% of initial specific capacity). The CQDs/Co3O4//AC HSC exhibits outstanding cycling stability in 1.6V and delivers 74.8Whkg−1 at 810.0Wkg−1. The CQDs played a crucial role in improving the exceptional electrochemical performance. The larger surface area of the composites could provide enough active sites for Faraday redox reactions, leading to the enhancement of the specific capacity. Furthermore, the presence of CQDs can enhance the electronic conductivity of the CQDs/MxOy composites, which could prove the fast transfer of electrons and facilitates the electrochemical reaction, thus enhancing the electrochemical performance. The facile synthetic strategy can be widely applicable for high output of other 3D porous hierarchical electrode materials with promising applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support by National Natural Science Foundation of China (51402362, 21471160), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (ZR2014EMQ012, ZR2016BM12), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (15CX08010A, 16CX05016, and 16CX05014A), and the starting-up fund from TJUT.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2017.05.127.
